signal_breakdown.signal: Weekly drop cadence with sold-out posts
signal_breakdown.explanation: Consistent Friday drops that sell out signals real demand and operational readiness for a platform.
Manual payment collection is the exact friction Hotplate eliminates. High switch motivation.
Large engaged audience well above the 1k-50k sweet spot, all feeding into a clunky DM flow.
Clear brand identity and differentiated product means loyal repeat buyers, not one-time curiosity.
Operating across two neighborhoods shows growth ambition beyond a single-spot hobbyist.

sfveganpops.recommended_action: Top priority outreach. Lead with the Friday sold-out chaos and offer a 10-minute Hotplate setup call.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Peak demand signal. Customers want in faster than the Google Form allows.
Spreadsheet-based order management breaks at scale. Classic switch-ready indicator.
Established audience with recurring drop rhythm. Infrastructure is the bottleneck, not demand.
Running a manual waitlist means they have overflow demand they cannot currently capture.
Premium positioning and named flavors suggest a real brand, not a side project.

nocrumbscookies.recommended_action: Reach out leading with the 2-hour sellout and the waitlist DM burden. Hotplate captures that overflow automatically.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Large established audience with recurring purchase behavior. High-value profile.
Already platformized but paying Square fees. Hotplate's lower take rate is a direct cost argument.
High AOV means Hotplate's flat fee model is proportionally cheaper than Square's percentage cut.
Artificial scarcity and quick sellouts match Hotplate's drop-format exactly.
Square gives them a working checkout. The switch pitch needs to be economic, not functional.

sfpastrylab.recommended_action: Lead with the fee comparison. Calculate their monthly Square cost and show Hotplate's equivalent. The math sells itself.

signal_breakdown.signal: Weekly Saturday pickups with sold-out posts
signal_breakdown.explanation: Recurring weekly rhythm with consistent sellouts. Textbook switch-ready operation.
Split-payment friction (some Venmo, some Zelle) and manual DM confirmation is painful at scale.
Already running a pre-order system manually. Hotplate is just an automated version of what they do.
Solid audience and established local identity. Not a hobbyist, not yet a business.
Zero digital infrastructure outside Instagram makes Hotplate a complete upgrade, not a migration.

bayareabao.recommended_action: Show them how Hotplate replicates their 48-hour pre-order window without the DM inbox management.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Has overflow demand she cannot fulfill. Waitlist signals capacity constraint, not demand constraint.
Managing orders and payments through two separate apps is exactly what Hotplate collapses into one.
100% sellthrough weekly. Demand is real and recurring.
Solid local following in a strong food neighborhood. Audience is ready to transact on a real platform.
Shows some digital organization, but Linktree linking to nothing order-related is a missed conversion.

sfsourdoughsarah.recommended_action: Lead with the waitlist. She is leaving money on the table every week. Hotplate captures it.

signal_breakdown.signal: Weekly Friday drops, sells out in 3 hours
signal_breakdown.explanation: Consistent fast sellouts mean demand outpaces her current ordering capacity.
Google Form orders require manual follow-up and payment collection. High friction for both sides.
Mid-size local audience with neighborhood identity. Growth is organic and community-driven.
Rotating seasonal menu shows a maker thinking like a food business, not a hobby baker.

sffocacciaco.recommended_action: Reach out after the next sold-out post. The timing makes the pitch land naturally.

signal_breakdown.signal: Weekly weekend drops with engaged community
signal_breakdown.explanation: Recurring drops with a loyal Filipino food community. Demand is proven and repeat.
Running orders through Facebook group is fragile. Members come and go. Hotplate is a permanent storefront.
Double-platform friction (Facebook to DM to Venmo) creates drop-off at each step.
Strong audience for the niche. Community trust is built. Infrastructure is the gap.
Slightly outside the SF core, which may affect reach but also means less competition for the neighborhood.

kusinafilipino.recommended_action: Acknowledge the Facebook community as a strength. Pitch Hotplate as the order layer that sits under that community, not a replacement.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Scarcity and limited-run drops are Hotplate's native format. This maker already thinks in drops.
Large established audience. The following exists. The question is whether the platform matches the ambition.
Shopify works but is built for always-on inventory. Drop-format selling is an awkward fit that Hotplate solves natively.
Owned domain and Shopify signal serious business intent, not a casual hobby.
Less frequent drops mean switch motivation may be lower. The pitch needs to address throughput, not chaos.

sfchocolatier.recommended_action: Lead with the drop-format fit. Hotplate is purpose-built for exactly what they already do. Shopify is the wrong tool.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Regular cadence with form-based ordering. Low hanging fruit for a Hotplate switch.
Moderate following with a neighborhood identity. Growing but not yet at peak switch-urgency.

signal_breakdown.signal: Korean banchan niche with loyal repeat buyers
signal_breakdown.explanation: Banchan is a staple purchase, not an impulse buy. Repeat buyer base is a strong foundation.

signal_breakdown.signal: Biweekly cadence is slower than weekly
signal_breakdown.explanation: Less frequent drops mean less operational chaos. Switch motivation exists but is not crisis-level.

sfkoreanbanchan.recommended_action: Warm outreach with a comparison of their current Google Form flow versus a Hotplate drop. No hard sell needed.

signal_breakdown.signal: Monthly drops with IG story ordering
signal_breakdown.explanation: Story-based ordering disappears after 24 hours. Customers miss the window constantly. Hotplate fixes this.
Solid niche following. The audience is there but the order infrastructure is flimsy.
Running demand surveys manually before each drop is exactly what Hotplate's pre-order waitlist automates.
Monthly frequency means lower operational pressure. Switch motivation is present but not urgent.

mochimochisf.recommended_action: Pitch the story-ordering problem specifically. 24-hour story windows lose customers every drop.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Already e-commerce enabled. Hotplate would be a narrower fit since their model is not SF-pickup-based.
Drop cadence aligns with Hotplate's model but shipping focus makes the fit less direct.
Reasonable audience but the national shipping model diverges from Hotplate's local pickup strength.
Hotplate's core UX is built around local pickup drops. This maker's model is a partial fit only.

hotpinksauce_sf.recommended_action: Lower priority. The national shipping model is a partial fit. Pitch the SF local pickup channel as an add-on, not a replacement.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Manual DM ordering is a clear pain point. Switch motivation exists once they know a solution exists.
Community trust is built. A platform switch would not lose customers. It would retain them better.
Smaller audience means less urgency but also means Hotplate could help them grow, not just manage scale.

signal_breakdown.signal: Occasional rather than strict weekly cadence
signal_breakdown.explanation: Irregular scheduling reduces operational pressure. Not yet at the breaking point.

tamalera_sf.recommended_action: Gentle outreach. The pain is real but the scale is still manageable. Offer Hotplate as a professionalization step.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Same ephemeral ordering problem as other story-based sellers. Orders are lost when the story expires.
Good audience size but the weather-dependent model limits reliable drop cadence.
No fixed cadence means no predictable customer habit. Hotplate works best with recurring drops.
Good location but seasonal-only operation limits Hotplate ROI.

sfcreamery_pop.recommended_action: Deprioritize for now. Revisit in spring when popup season ramps up and the ordering chaos is active.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Custom cake pricing does not fit Hotplate's fixed-price drop model well. Friction is different in nature.
Solid audience but custom-order model means no drop cadence to anchor a Hotplate switch around.
Order-by-DM custom work is hard to platformize. Hotplate is not the right fit for bespoke orders.
Good location, but without a recurring drop, the Hotplate use case is weak.

cakebychance_sf.recommended_action: Not a fit right now. Custom cake ordering does not map to Hotplate's drop format. Flag for a future box of the month type offering.

signal_breakdown.signal: Monthly Off the Grid popup via Eventbrite
signal_breakdown.explanation: Using an established venue and ticketing system. Less ordering chaos since Eventbrite handles it.
Smaller audience and infrequent drops put this below the high-value threshold.
One popup per month through a venue is not the self-managed drop model Hotplate is built for.
Already using a platform. The switch case requires a clear upgrade argument, not just an alternative.

theempanadadiaries.recommended_action: Skip for now. They have an existing event platform and low drop frequency. Check back if they go independent.

signal_breakdown.explanation: Weekly cadence and DM ordering are the right signals, but the audience size limits switch urgency.
Below the 1k-50k sweet spot's productive range. Growth is needed before platform switch makes sense.
Neighborhood presence but small scale suggests early-stage hobbyist rather than high-value maker.
Menu experimentation is a good sign long term, but at this scale it reads as hobby exploration.

breadandbutter_sf.recommended_action: Watch and revisit at 5k followers. The behavior is right but the scale is not there yet.
